title: The Vienna Game & Gambit
subtitle: 1.e4 e5 2.Nc3 — a sound, aggressive system: build the f4 break, punish the careless, hunt the king.

concepts.name: 2.Nc3 — the Vienna knight
concepts.short: Develop, guard e4, and keep the f-pawn free so you can play f4 later.

concepts.name: The Vienna Gambit (f4)
concepts.short: f4 hits e5, opens the f-file, and aims your pieces at f7 and the Black king.

concepts.name: e5! — harry the knight
concepts.short: If Black grabs on f4 with the e-pawn, e5 kicks the f6-knight and seizes space with tempo.

concepts.name: ...d5 — the equalizer
concepts.short: Black's correct reply to the gambit: strike the centre back with ...d5, not grab on f4.

concepts.name: Bishop's-Opening order (Bc4)
concepts.short: Bc4 and Nc3 can come in either order; aim the bishop at f7 and watch for ...Nxe4 traps.

concepts.name: The f-file attack
concepts.short: After fxe5 the f-file opens; rook to f1, queen toward g3/h4 — the whole army points at the king.

lessons.title: The Vienna Knight
lessons.summary: Why 2.Nc3, and the quiet way to play it.

intro.text: Before theory, a feel for the positions this opening creates. Solve these — they all come from real Vienna games.
After 1.e4 e5 White must look after the e4-pawn. Viktor shows you the two faces of the Vienna.

coach.text: 1.e4 e5. Most players reach for 2.Nf3, hitting e5 at once. The Vienna chooses 2.Nc3 instead — develop a piece, guard e4 from the side, and crucially leave the f-pawn unblocked.
Here is the whole point. With the knight on c3 (not f3), the f-pawn is free. White can now prepare f4 — a pawn-storm against e5 that 2.Nf3 can never play. That is the Vienna's hidden weapon.

You don't have to gambit. The quiet Vienna — Bc4 and d3 — is a calm, solid Italian-style setup: bishop eyeing f7, ready to castle. Sound and easy to play. But the f4 break is what makes the Vienna sting.
Play the Vienna move: develop the queenside knight so it guards e4 and frees the f-pawn.

successLine.text: Exactly — 2.Nc3. e4 is covered and f4 is on the menu.

retryLine.text: The Vienna is defined by one knight move: Nb1–c3.

intro.text: One more from a Vienna middlegame. Spot the shot.

lessons.title: The Vienna Gambit
lessons.summary: 3.f4 — what it threatens and the model line.

intro.text: After 2...Nc6 or 2...Nf6, White unleashes 3.f4. Viktor explains what this pawn is really doing.

coach.text: 3.f4. Three threats at once: it attacks e5; if you ever play fxe5 the f-file rips open toward f7; and White grabs the centre. This is a real gambit — White will often give the f-pawn to get a raging attack.

Black's best answer is NOT to take, but to hit back in the centre: 3...d5. Counter a wing strike with a centre strike — a rule worth carving in stone. Grabbing on f4 hands White exactly the attack he wants.

Main line: 4.fxe5 Nxe4. Both sides have grabbed a centre pawn; the position is roughly level and very sharp. White will develop Nf3, d4, and use the half-open f-file. Know this picture cold — it's the heart of the gambit.

intro.text: Play it through once with Viktor talking you past each move.

coach.text: 2.Nc3 — the Vienna knight.
3.f4 — the gambit. Pressure on e5 and the f-file looms.
3...d5! Black declines correctly and strikes the centre.
4.fxe5 — capture, opening the f-file.
4...Nxe4 — Black recaptures the centre pawn. We've reached the main tabiya.
5.Nf3 — calm development. Next d4, Bd3/Be2, 0-0, and the rook lands on f1 staring at f7. A balanced, fighting game where White knows the plan and Black often does not.
Black has played 2...Nf6. Unleash the Vienna Gambit.

successLine.text: 3.f4 — e5 is hit, the f-file is loaded, and f7 is in the crosshairs.

retryLine.text: The gambit is one pawn push: f2–f4, striking e5.

lessons.title: Punish the Pawn-Grab
lessons.summary: When Black takes on f4, e5! kicks the knight and wins big.

intro.text: Many opponents greedily play 3...exf4. Viktor shows why that's a mistake.

coach.text: Black took the bait: 3...exf4?. The f6-knight no longer has the e5-pawn to lean on, and White has a powerful in-between idea before recovering the pawn.

4.e5! With tempo. The pawn kicks the f6-knight and grabs a big space chunk in the centre. Black must move the knight to an awkward square — every retreat is a small disaster. White is comfortably better.
The worst try is 4...Ng4?, hoping to bother f2. But the knight is now undefended on g4 — and White has a queen.
Black grabbed with 3...exf4. Don't rush to recapture — punish the loose knight first.

successLine.text: 4.e5! The knight is kicked and White seizes the centre with tempo. You'll round up f4 later.

retryLine.text: Look for a move that gains a tempo. The e-pawn can attack the f6-knight: e4–e5.

coach.text: Black lashed out with 4...Ng4?. The knight has no defender. Win it.

successLine.text: 5.Qxg4! A whole piece for nothing. The lesson: a knight that lands on an undefended square in front of a queen is just lost.

retryLine.text: What attacks g4 and is worth more than a knight? The queen on d1 looks straight at it.

intro.text: The same instinct — pounce on undefended pieces — in fresh Vienna positions.
When Black defends correctly with 3...d5, you must know the plan rather than a trap. Viktor lays it out.

coach.text: 5.Nf3, the tabiya. Material is level (each side took one centre pawn). Black's knight sits proudly on e4 but it can be challenged; White's trumps are the half-open f-file and the e5-pawn cramping Black.
6.d4 anchors e5 and opens lines for the c1-bishop. The plan from here is simple and strong: Bd3 to hit e4, Be3 or Bg5, castle, and double on the f-file. Every piece has an obvious good square.
Bd3 challenges the e4-knight directly and lines the bishop up toward h7. If Black trades on c3, bxc3 hands White a broad pawn centre and the open b-file. White has an easy, pressing game.
You are Black for a moment, to feel both sides. Black's natural developing move here strikes from the side — bring the dark-squared bishop out actively.

successLine.text: Good — a sensible bishop move (Bc5 is the engine's pick; Bb4 and Be7 are also fine). Black develops and prepares to castle. The position is balanced and rich.

retryLine.text: Develop the f8-bishop to an active square (c5, b4 or e7) and head for castling.

coach.text: Back to White. Black's knight is planted on e4. Develop your light bishop to the square that both attacks that knight and aims at the kingside.

successLine.text: 7.Bd3! It hits the e4-knight and points at h7 — exactly the multi-purpose move the position wants.

retryLine.text: Which bishop development attacks e4 AND eyes h7? The f1-bishop to d3.

intro.text: Tactics that arise from these structures — kingside pressure pays off.

lessons.title: Move-Order & the f7 Trap
lessons.summary: Bishop's-Opening order, and the Frankenstein-Dracula refutation of a greedy ...Nxe4.

segments.title: Bc4 first, and a famous trap

intro.text: Nc3 and Bc4 are interchangeable. Knowing both orders lets you reach the Vienna against many move-orders — and set a deadly trap.

coach.text: Bishop's-Opening order: 2.Bc4 (or 2.Nc3 then 3.Bc4). Same pieces, same plan — bishop drilling at f7, knight guarding e4. If you like the quiet Vienna you can almost always steer into this.

The trap — known as the Frankenstein-Dracula: after 2.Nc3 Nf6 3.Bc4, if Black snatches 3...Nxe4??, White plays 4.Qh5!! — double attack. The queen hits the loose e4-knight AND threatens Qxf7# (the bishop covers f7). Black is already in deep trouble.

If Black panics with 4...Nf6?? to defend f7, it's mate in one: f7 is still only defended by the king. The queen crashes in. Black's best is 4...Nd6 — it blocks the bishop and gives the piece back, after which the game is roughly equal but Black was the one forced to grovel. (4...g6 also defends, but stays passive; other tries leave White clearly on top.)
Black greedily grabbed e4 with the knight. Find the double-attacking queen move that hits the knight and f7 at once.

successLine.text: 4.Qh5! Two threats at once: Qxe5+ forking off the e4-knight, and the lethal Qxf7#. Black's best is 4...Nd6, handing the piece back — after which the game is roughly equal but Black had to grovel. (4...g6 also holds, but passively; greedier tries leave White clearly better.)

retryLine.text: Look for a queen move that attacks both e5 (and through it the e4-knight) and f7. Qd1–h5.

coach.text: Black tried 4...Nf6 to cover h5 — but forgot f7. Deliver mate.

successLine.text: 5.Qxf7#! The bishop on c4 guards the queen, the king has no flight square. A textbook scholar's-mate pattern hiding inside the Vienna.

retryLine.text: f7 is defended only by the king, and your bishop backs up the queen. Take it.

segments.title: Cool-down: mate on f7 and friends

intro.text: Attacks against the f7/king zone — the Vienna's favourite target.

lessons.title: The Attack & the Boss
lessons.summary: Tie the plans together, then play the Vienna against a real opponent.

segments.title: The whole plan in one picture

intro.text: Before you play, fix the Vienna's recurring ideas in your mind.

coach.text: A typical Vienna middlegame. Your checklist as White: 1) keep e5 supported, 2) trade or kick the e4-knight, 3) pile on the half-open f-file, 4) swing the queen to g3/h4 and the dark bishop to g5 — every piece points at the Black king.

Remember the two golden rules from this chapter: counter f4 with ...d5 (centre beats wing), and if your opponent grabs on f4, hit with e5! and chase the knight. Know these and you will out-prepare most opponents straight out of the opening.

segments.title: Boss: play the Vienna as White

bossIntro.text: You have e4 and e5 on the board and it is your move two. Play 2.Nc3, choose your weapon — quiet or gambit — and use everything you've learned: the f4 break, the e5 kick, the f-file, f7. Beat me.

questions.prompt: What is the defining idea behind 2.Nc3 in the Vienna, rather than the usual 2.Nf3?
options: It pins Black's knight to the king
It guards e4 while leaving the f-pawn free to play f4
It prepares to castle queenside at once
questions.explain: The c3-knight defends e4 from the side, so White keeps the f-pawn unblocked and can launch f4 — the Vienna's signature break that 2.Nf3 can never play.

questions.prompt: White has played the Vienna Gambit with 3.f4. What is Black's best reply?
questions.explain: Counter a wing strike with a centre strike: 3...d5! is the equalizer. Grabbing with 3...exf4 hands White the e5! kick and a strong attack.

questions.prompt: What is the single biggest reason White is happy when Black answers the gambit with 3...exf4 instead of 3...d5?
options: White can immediately castle into safety
Black's f6-knight has lost the e5-pawn behind it, so e5! kicks it with tempo
White wins the f4-pawn back by force next move
Black can no longer develop the f8-bishop
questions.explain: With e5 gone, nothing stops the e4–e5 thrust: it hits the f6-knight with tempo and grabs the centre. Recapturing on f4 can wait.

questions.prompt: After 2.Nc3 Nf6 3.Bc4 Nxe4?? 4.Qh5, what makes Qh5 so strong?
options: It pins the e4-knight to the king
It double-attacks: Qxe5+ wins the e4-knight, and Qxf7# is mate (the c4-bishop guards f7)
It threatens to win Black's queen with discovered check
It defends e4 and prepares to castle
questions.explain: Qh5 hits two things at once — the e5-pawn (and through it the e4-knight, by Qxe5+) and f7, where Qxf7# is mate because the bishop on c4 backs the queen. Black's best is 4...Nd6, giving the piece back for a roughly equal game; greedier tries leave White clearly better.

questions.prompt: Bishop's-Opening move-order: you opened 2.Bc4 and Black replied 2...Nf6. Steer the game into the Vienna — make the move that does the Vienna's job.
questions.explain: 3.Nc3 transposes to the Vienna: the knight guards e4 from the side (vital now that Black eyes ...Nxe4) and the f-pawn stays free for a later f4. Nc3 and Bc4 are interchangeable — either order reaches the same setup.

questions.prompt: After 3...exf4 4.e5, Black retreated the wrong way with 4...Ne4?. Find White's reply.
questions.explain: 5.Nxe4! simply wins the piece — the knight fled to a square covered by White's own knight. Same lesson as Ng4: a knight that lands where it is attacked and undefended is just lost.

questions.prompt: Bishop's-Opening trap: after 4.Qh5 Black tried 4...d6, defending e5 but ignoring f7. White to move.
questions.explain: 5.Qxf7# — the c4-bishop guards the queen and the king has no flight square. Defending e5 does nothing about the mate on f7.

questions.prompt: Bishop-first order: 2.Bc4 Nf6 3.Nc3 Nxe4?? and White answered with Qd1-h5. Judge White's move.
questions.explain: Correct — the Frankenstein-Dracula double-attack, and it works whatever the move order. Qh5 hits the e5-pawn (so Qxe5+ rounds up the e4-knight) and threatens Qxf7#, since the c4-bishop backs the queen. Black must grovel with 4...Nd6 to give the piece back.

questions.prompt: A Vienna Gambit middlegame: White has the big e4+d4 centre and Black has just retreated a knight to c6. The f6-knight has nothing behind it. White to move — use the same idea from this chapter.
questions.explain: e5! kicks the f6-knight with tempo and rolls the pawn centre forward — the exact 'harry the knight' idea, now in a different position. The knight has no good square and White seizes the initiative.

questions.prompt: Against the gambit, Black answered 3.f4 with 3...d5. Judge the move.
questions.explain: Correct. 3...d5 is the principled equalizer — meeting a wing thrust with a central counter. The game is balanced and sharp.

questions.prompt: Against the gambit, Black tried the early 3...Qh4+. Judge the check.
questions.explain: Blunder. White simply plays 4.g3, hitting the queen with tempo; it retreats and White follows with fxe5, emerging a clean tempo up with a strong centre. The premature check only helps White develop.

questions.prompt: Bishop's-Opening Vienna: 2.Nc3 Nf6 3.Bc4, and Black has lashed out with 3...Ng4?, jumping at f2. White to move — make Black pay for the loose knight.
questions.explain: Qxg4! The knight sat undefended on g4, square-staring straight at the queen on d1. The same lesson as the gambit's 4...Ng4? — a knight that lands where it is attacked and undefended is simply lost.

questions.prompt: In the Frankenstein-Dracula trap, after 4.Qh5 Black played the best defence, 4...Nd6. Judge it.
questions.explain: Correct — and best. 4...Nd6 blocks the bishop and returns the piece to stop Qxf7#; Black equalizes, though he was the one forced to grovel. (4...g6 also holds; the greedy alternatives all leave White clearly better.)

questions.prompt: Main-line tabiya: White has just played the developing move Bf1-d3. Judge White's idea.
questions.explain: Correct. Bd3 attacks the e4-knight and aims at h7 — a textbook multi-purpose developing move in this structure.
